intro:
Ezra (Hebrew Ezra, "help") was a priest and scribe who led a return of Jewish exiles from Babylon to Jerusalem and spearheaded the spiritual reform of the restored community. He was of the highest priestly lineage — a direct descendant of Aaron through Seraiah, the last chief priest before the Babylonian exile (Ezra 7:1–5) — yet he is remembered above all as "a scribe skilled in the Law of Moses" (Ezra 7:6). In him the offices of priest and scholar of Scripture converge, marking the shift in post-exilic Judaism from temple and prophet toward the teaching and keeping of the written word.

Ezra's mission belongs to the era after the first return under Zerubbabel, when the temple had been rebuilt but the people's covenant life had grown lax. In the seventh year of Artaxerxes I (about 458 BC) Ezra came up from Babylon with a company of exiles and a royal commission granting him authority and treasures to beautify the house of God and to appoint judges to teach and enforce the law of God (Ezra 7:11–26). Before setting out he proclaimed a fast at the river Ahava, ashamed to ask the king for soldiers because he had testified that "the hand of our God is for good on all who seek him"; the company entrusted the perilous journey, with its silver and gold, to God's protection (Ezra 8:21–23).

On arriving he was confronted with the crisis of intermarriage with the surrounding peoples, which threatened to dissolve the distinct, holy community God had preserved. Ezra's response was not first command but contrition: he tore his garments and poured out a great prayer of confession, identifying himself with the nation's guilt (Ezra 9:5–15), and the people covenanted to put away the foreign marriages (Ezra 10:1–4). Alongside Nehemiah a generation later, Ezra brought the Book of the Law before the assembled people at the Water Gate, reading from morning till midday while the Levites "gave the sense, so that the people understood"; the nation wept, then was bidden to rejoice, and kept the Feast of Booths and renewed the covenant (Nehemiah 8:1–12; Nehemiah 8:18).

Ezra's enduring significance lies in his settled purpose: "Ezra had set his heart to study the Law of the LORD, and to do it, and to teach his statutes and rules in Israel" (Ezra 7:10) — study, obedience, and teaching held together as a model of godliness. Jewish tradition honoured him as the restorer of the Scriptures after the exile, linking him with the gathering of the canon and the Great Synagogue, and even saying that had Moses not preceded him, Ezra would have been worthy to receive the Law. By recalling the people to the word of God and guarding the integrity of the covenant community, Ezra helped preserve the line and faith of Israel through which, in the fullness of time, the Messiah would come.
